stage: Round of 32 (knockout stage — first knockout match analyzed this season)

South Africa.status: Clear underdog today (Canada favored on every market)

Canada.group_record: 1W-1D-1L, 4pts, GD+5 (driven by a 6-0 demolition of Qatar)
Canada.status: Favored on every market; home-continent advantage at SoFi Stadium

RSA.DISCIPLINE_FLAG: 2 red cards in a single match (vs Mexico) plus 1-2 YC every game — a genuinely ill-disciplined team, not just a noisy sample. Elevates confidence on card-heavy questions today (Q1, Q14).

CAN.BIMODAL_FLAG: Corner average (11.67) is heavily skewed by the 19-corner Qatar blowout — same pattern as Algeria's bimodal SOT/corners in ALG-AUT (2026-06-27). Competitive-match corner output is really 7-9, not 11.67.
CAN.DAVID_PRECEDENT: Jonathan David scored a hat-trick in the CAN 6-0 QAT blowout (29', 45'+3', 90'+2') — direct, real precedent for Q12 (any player 2+ goals) that the research agent independently surfaced and verified via ESPN keyEvents/rosters. Real but should not be over-weighted: that was an extreme mismatch (Qatar), today's opponent is tougher.

Goal_1_time_bracket.NOTE: directly answers Q5 (goal before first hydration break) — sum brackets up to the break minute

context_flags: FIRST_KNOCKOUT_MATCH: every question is scoped to 'regulation (90 min + stoppage)' except Q9 (advance), reflecting the new ET/penalties possibility. Smarkets correctly distinguishes a 'To qualify' market (no draw, 147758349) from the regulation-scoped 'Full-time result' (147758216) — used Q9 from the former, everything else from the regulation-scoped equivalents.

HYDRATION_BREAK_NOW_MANDATORY: per dedicated research (rsa_can_2026-06-28_new_question_types_sources.md, Section A.1), FIFA made hydration breaks unconditional for every WC2026 match regardless of venue/weather, and 5/5 prior matches at this exact venue (SoFi Stadium) confirm it empirically (~23-25' and ~67-70'). Used 24' and 69' as the representative break minutes for Q1/Q3/Q5 modeling.

PLAYER_TO_SCORE_2PLUS_MARKET_IS_A_TRAP: summing all 44 individual illiquid offer-side player contracts gives 0.76, which is NOT a valid probability for Q12 — illiquid offer-side overpricing compounds badly across many contracts. Used a proper Poisson/binomial decomposition off the TEAM goal markets instead (see Q12 reasoning). Flagging this so the same mistake (summing many illiquid offers) isn't repeated on a future multi-contract market.

RSA_DISCIPLINE: 2 red cards in one match plus consistent 1-2 YC/game — combined with Canada's own 1.67 YC/game average and the market's own elevated card lambda (~4.0, well above either team's individual history), this is a genuinely high-card-expectation match, consistent with the ARG-AUT-derived lesson that competitive/stakes-driven matches produce more cards than dominant-win comparisons suggest.

NO_STRUCTURED_SOURCE_FOR_OFFSIDE_TIMING: confirmed gap from the research agent — ESPN's keyEvents has no discrete offside event type, so Q3 has no direct market or structured-data anchor. Modeled via ESPN team-average Poisson scaled to the pre-break time window, with a downward trim for a cautious knockout-match start; treat this estimate as lower-confidence than the rest.

questions.text: Will a card be shown after the second hydration break, including any extra time?
questions.reasoning: No direct market for card timing exists beyond the 1st-10-minutes bracket. Backed out total-match card lambda (~4.0) from 3 separate O/U card thresholds (consistent fits: 3.87/4.01/3.99). Assumed ~40% of cards land in the final ~25 min of regulation (69' onward) given known late-match card clustering (fatigue, time-wasting, professional fouls) — gives λ_post69≈1.58, P(≥1)=0.79. Added the chance of an additional card in extra time (28.3% chance ET happens × ~55% chance of a card in a 30-min knockout ET period, applied only if no card already occurred). Combined: 0.83, trimmed to account for the timing-share assumption not being directly market-verified.

questions.text: Will both teams score in regulation (90 minutes + stoppage time)?
Will either team be ruled offside before the first hydration break?
questions.reasoning: No structured source exists (confirmed gap — see context_flags). Combined ESPN offside average (RSA 2.33 + CAN 1.67 = 4.0 full match), scaled to the ~24-minute pre-break window (24/95≈25% of match) gives a naive λ≈1.01, P(≥1)=0.64. Trimmed down for the typically cautious opening exchanges of a high-stakes knockout match (teams probing rather than committing players forward early), and for the general lack of market anchor.

questions.text: Will a substitute score a goal in regulation?
questions.reasoning: No direct market. Used the market-implied total-goal lambda (2.34, from O/U2.5/O/U3.5 goals) combined with a standard ~13% per-goal substitute-scorer share, giving P(≥1 sub goal) ≈ 1-(1-0.13)^2.34 ≈ 0.27. Canada has a concrete precedent this tournament (Nathan Saliba scored as a substitute in the 6-0 Qatar win), supporting the base rate rather than requiring an upward adjustment beyond it.

questions.text: Will a goal be scored before the first hydration break?
questions.reasoning: Smarkets' 'Goal 1 time bracket' market directly answers this: P(first goal ≤24') = bracket(1-10) + bracket(11-20) + 40% of bracket(21-30) [partial credit for the ~24' break landing inside that bracket] = 0.1768+0.1666+0.4×0.1367 = 0.398.

questions.text: Will there be 9 or more total corner kicks in regulation?
questions.reasoning: Direct market: Over8.5 corners = 55.93%. Trust it over Canada's skewed 11.67 ESPN average (see BIMODAL_FLAG) — the market already reflects their real competitive-match output.

questions.text: Will regulation (90 minutes + stoppage time) end in a tie?
Will Canada be ahead at halftime?
questions.reasoning: Direct HT market: Canada = 37.75%. The high HT-draw price (46.74%) suppresses this below Canada's full-match favorite status, consistent with a cautious knockout-match start.

questions.text: Will South Africa advance to the Round of 16?
questions.reasoning: Smarkets 'To qualify' market (the correct knockout-scoped market, distinct from the regulation-only FTR): South Africa = 26.86%.

questions.text: Will a penalty kick be awarded during regulation?
Will the second half produce more goals than the first half in regulation, excluding extra time?
questions.reasoning: Direct market: 'Highest scoring half' → Second Half = 41.21%. Exactly matches the question's wording.

questions.text: Will any player score more than 1 goal (excluding own goals) in regulation?
questions.pick_tier: MEDIUM CONFIDENCE — MODELED, MARKET-SUM TRAP AVOIDED
questions.reasoning: The literal Smarkets 'Player to score 2+ goals' market sums to 0.76 across 44 illiquid offer-side contracts — not usable (see PLAYER_TO_SCORE_2PLUS_MARKET_IS_A_TRAP flag). Instead modeled properly: backed out team goal lambdas from the team O/U goal markets (SA≈0.80, CAN≈1.54), then used a Poisson-binomial decomposition assuming a top-scorer goal-share of ~38% for Canada (multiple attacking outlets) and ~45% for South Africa (more reliant on a single forward when they do score) to get P(team brace). Combined: P(CAN brace)=0.118, P(SA brace)=0.051, P(any)=0.163. Nudged up slightly for Canada's concrete hat-trick precedent this tournament (Jonathan David, CAN-QAT) without over-weighting an extreme-mismatch outlier game.

questions.text: Will Cyle Larin score a goal (excluding own goals) in regulation?
Will there be 4 or more total cards shown in regulation?
questions.reasoning: Direct market: Over3.5 cards = 56.88%, consistent with South Africa's own poor disciplinary record (2 red cards in one match alone) and the market's elevated implied card lambda (~4.0) relative to either team's individual season average.

questions.text: Will Iqraam Rayners have at least 1 shot on target in regulation?

strongest_picks: Q1: Card after 2nd hydration break, incl. ET = 0.78 (HIGH CONFIDENCE — high market-implied card lambda combined with South Africa's confirmed poor disciplinary record)
Q6: 9+ corners = 0.56 (HIGH CONFIDENCE — direct market, correctly overrides Canada's blowout-skewed ESPN corner average)

Q12: Any player brace = 0.18 (MEDIUM CONFIDENCE — avoided a real trap: the naive sum of 44 illiquid player contracts gave a badly wrong 0.76; proper team-level Poisson/binomial decomposition gives 0.18)

Q1.question: Card after 2nd hydration break, incl ET?
Q1.notes: WORST RESULT. Our self-constructed timing-decomposition (total-card lambda 4.0 x 40% post-69' share + ET add-on) was badly wrong. Combined with Q14 also missing low, actual total match cards were apparently very low (likely 0-2), well below what the O/U card market's own implied lambda suggested. A derived sub-question built on an unverified timing-share assumption carries much more risk than a direct market, even when the base-rate market (cards O/U) pointed a real direction.

Q8.question: Canada ahead at HT?
Q8.notes: Confirms the only goal came in the 2H (consistent with Q11 outcome) — our RULE8-style HT suppression below Canada's full-match favorite status was correct.

Q11.question: 2H more goals than 1H?
Q11.notes: The single match goal came in the 2H. Both we and the direct 'Highest scoring half' market (41.21%) underpriced this relative to the crowd's better-calibrated 49%.

Q12.question: Any player 2+ goals?
Q12.notes: METHODOLOGY WIN. Directly vindicates avoiding the illiquid-44-contract-sum trap (which gave a misleading 0.76) in favor of a proper team-level Poisson/binomial brace decomposition.

Q14.question: 4+ total cards?
Q14.notes: Paired with Q1 — actual total cards were lower than our market-lambda-implied fit (4.0) suggested. The card O/U market itself may have been pricing in extra-time-inclusive card risk that didn't materialize since the match never went to ET.

Q15.question: Rayners 1+ SOT in regulation?
Q15.notes: SECOND-WORST RESULT. Trusted an illiquid offer-side player SOT price near-raw (0.52) despite South Africa being a 17.7% extreme underdog who ultimately lost. The crowd (32%) was much better calibrated. Unlike the Sabitzer/ARG-AUT case (where a competitive underdog's creative player was correctly NOT suppressed), South Africa's own team SOT average all tournament was already low (3.33/game) — team-level shot-shyness should be weighted ahead of the generic 'competitive underdog = more shots' heuristic when the team's underlying attacking output is already weak.
